proposals.title: EMPTY_KEY Sentinel vs memset(0x80) Mismatch — Immediate Infinite Loop
proposals.detect: Open-addressing hash table where EMPTY_KEY = INT32_MIN (0x80000000) but init uses memset(keys, 0x80, sizeof(keys)) which fills each 4-byte slot with 0x80808080 ≠ INT32_MIN. The probe loop `while (keys[s] != EMPTY_KEY && keys[s] != key)` never terminates because un-touched slots never satisfy `keys[s] == EMPTY_KEY`.
proposals.fix: Either: (a) Replace `memset(keys, 0x80, sizeof(keys))` with `std::fill(keys, keys + CAP, INT32_MIN)` so slots are truly initialized to EMPTY_KEY, OR (b) Change `EMPTY_KEY` to `(int32_t)0x80808080` which IS the value memset(0x80) produces for int32_t. Option (a) is preferred for clarity. Rule: the memset(0x80) shortcut is only valid when the sentinel is exactly 0x80808080 for int32_t (or 0x8080808080808080 for int64_t).
proposals.evidence: Q18 timed out on all 6 iterations. Code had LocalMap, MergeMap, QualSet all using memset(0x80) + EMPTY_KEY=INT32_MIN. Every thread spun infinitely on the first insert during Phase 1 lineitem scan.

proposals.title: Optimizer Regresses Past Best — Final Iteration Code Is Not Always Best
proposals.detect: optimization_history shows `improved=false` for multiple trailing iterations while timing increases (e.g., Q9: best 607 ms at iter_1, final 870 ms at iter_5; Q1: best 57 ms at iter_4, final 68 ms at iter_5). The framework executes the last iteration's binary for benchmarking, not the best-found binary.
proposals.fix: The code generator / optimizer loop should: (1) Track the best-performing passing iteration by wall-clock timing. (2) After 2 consecutive non-improving iterations, stop further exploration. (3) Report and use the best iteration's binary for final benchmarking — not the last attempted. Alternatively, copy the best binary to a canonical output path after each improvement.
proposals.evidence: Q9 regressed from 607 ms (iter_1, improved=true) to 870 ms (iter_5, improved=false). Q1 regressed from 57 ms (iter_4) to 68 ms (iter_5). Q3 plateaued for 4 iterations without improvement.

proposals.title: Phase-Level Optimization Without Total-Time Guard — Work Shifting Between Phases
proposals.detect: One phase improves significantly while another phase degrades by a similar amount, leaving total execution time unchanged. Example: Q3 optimizer improved main_scan by ~97 ms but degraded dim_filter by ~70 ms across iterations 1-5 — net improvement was only ~13 ms total across 5 optimization rounds.
proposals.fix: When the optimizer proposes changes targeting a specific phase, it must enforce that the TOTAL wall-clock time improves. Phase-level timing breakdowns in [TIMING] output should be used to detect when a proposed optimization merely shifts work rather than eliminating it. Flag any iteration where the targeted phase improves but total time does not improve by at least 5%.
proposals.evidence: Q3 dim_filter: 35 ms (iter_1) → 105 ms (iter_5). Q3 main_scan: 359 ms (iter_1) → 262 ms (iter_5). Net total: 799 ms → 786 ms — 1.7% improvement despite 5 iterations of optimization.

notes: Q18 failed on ALL 6 iterations due to memset(0x80)/INT32_MIN sentinel mismatch causing infinite loop at 0% load factor. New experience entry C20 proposed.
Q9 found best solution at iter_1 (607ms) but final iter_5 code regressed to 870ms (43% worse). Framework should use best-iteration binary, not last-iteration binary.
Q3 plateaued at ~786ms; dim_filter phase degraded 3x across optimization while main_scan improved — net benefit minimal. Experience entry P12 proposed for phase-shift detection.
Q6 converged optimally in just 2 iterations (170ms → 22ms, 7.8x). P2 fix (mmap zero-copy) was the dominant win.
Q1 converged to 68ms (3.7x improvement) with vectorized scan. Slight regression from best (57ms at iter_4) to final (68ms at iter_5).
